ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general veal food class; no specific 'veal leg, raw, lean' term exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers raw veal meat broadly; no leg-specific or lean-specific sub-code identified. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw lean veal leg as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of lean veal leg is approximately 100 g, representing a single portion before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Veal, leg, top round, separable lean only, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174367)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for veal, leg, top round, separable lean only, raw (FDC ID 174367) is the primary approved source for this record. HPB Singapore values from the input record (energy 107 kcal, protein 21.28 g, fat 1.76 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g, sodium 64 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, potassium 372 mg, calcium 5 mg, iron 0.8 mg, cholesterol 78 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for this cut and are retained as primary values. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used to supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrient fields not reported in the HPB record. HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.sg) corroborates the core macronutrient values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174367)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / USDA FoodData Central
gi_source.source_match_notes: Veal leg, raw, lean contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174367). Pure protein and fat foods are not assigned a glycemic index value by the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, as GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate load of 50 g. Glycemic index is set to null (not_applicable) per Veritas protocol for foods with negligible carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI test exists or is needed for this food category.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (64 mg/100 g) and high potassium (372 mg/100 g) yield a strongly favourable Na:K ratio for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Lean veal leg is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt, high-sodium marinades, or processed sauces; grilling or roasting with herbs is preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (21.28 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lean veal leg is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes as a zero-carbohydrate, high-protein food; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid high-sugar glazes or sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.76 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (0.61 g/100 g) with high protein make this a lean red meat option; cholesterol is moderate at 78 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean veal leg is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet when consumed in moderate portions (100 g cooked) and prepared without added saturated fats; limit to 1–2 servings per week as part of a varied protein intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: As a red meat (bovine/calf), veal contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) that can amplify inflammatory pathways relevant to RA, despite its low fat content.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including veal to no more than 1–2 servings per week per EULAR guidance, and preferentially substitute with anti-inflammatory proteins such as oily fish or legumes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veal leg, raw, lean is the uncooked hind leg muscle meat of a young calf (veal), with all separable fat removed. It displays a characteristic pale pink to light red colour and has a fine-grained, tender texture compared with mature beef. As a raw ingredient, it is used in a wide range of culinary preparations including roasting, pan-searing, braising, and escalope dishes common in European cuisines such as Italian (vitello), French, and German cooking. Nutritionally, lean veal leg is a high-protein (21.28 g/100 g), very low-fat (1.76 g/100 g), zero-carbohydrate food providing 107 kcal per 100 g. Its saturated fat content is low at approximately 0.61 g/100 g, making it one of the leanest red meat options available. Sodium is low at 64 mg/100 g while potassium is high at 372 mg/100 g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. It provides meaningful amounts of B vitamins including niacin (7.16 mg), B6 (0.36 mg), B12 (1.3 mcg), riboflavin (0.26 mg), and zinc (2.57 mg). Cholesterol is moderate at 78 mg/100 g. Clinically, lean veal leg is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (zero glycaemic impact), hypertension (low sodium, high potassium), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat). As a red meat it should be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal when sourced from appropriately slaughtered animals.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for veal leg, separable lean only, raw (FDC ID 174367). Supplementary micronutrient values (saturated fat, B vitamins, minerals) drawn directly from USDA SR Legacy. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Exact food category match achieved.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: veal leg is a pure muscle meat with no carbohydrate content. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same rationale. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle food containing no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174367 for this cut). Vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in HPB record and USDA SR Legacy does not reliably report retinol activity equivalents for lean leg cuts. Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB record; USDA SR Legacy does not report vitamin D for this specific veal cut. Vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB record or USDA SR Legacy for this cut. Saturated fat (0.61 g), monounsaturated fat (0.55 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.14 g), and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174367 (veal, leg, top round, separable lean only, raw). Food subgroup corrected from 'Beef' to 'Veal' as veal is a distinct subcategory of bovine meat from young calves. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas protocol for pure protein/fat foods. The University of Sydney GI Database methodology requires a minimum 50 g available carbohydrate load for GI testing; this food does not meet that threshold.

Protocol-compliant handling confirmed. glycemic_index is null (not 0) as required.
